full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's too hot to be Advent, and yet this gospel reading is an Advent theme, Advent being that<|10.22|> <|10.22|> dark little short season just before Christmas during the winter hours, and as darkness seems<|18.26|> <|18.26|> to be the longest part of the day.<|21.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This theme, keep your lamps trimmed and burning, is perfect for Advent.<|5.34|> <|5.58|> And apparently, whoever planned or devised the lectionary thought, it's actually a theme<|10.56|> <|10.56|> for every season of our lives at all times and in all places.<|14.62|> <|14.86|> Keep your lamps trimmed and burning, awaiting the Master's return.<|19.72|> <|21.24|> But what exactly are we waiting for?<|24.98|> <|24.98|> What?<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In parts of the New Testament, we're admonished to wait for a literal return of Christ at the end of time,<|8.62|> <|9.36|> as time itself wraps up upon itself and exists no more.<|13.98|> <|14.10|> For example, Paul admonishes the Corinthians in no uncertain terms to maintain the status quo.<|21.88|> <|22.04|> Don't change a thing.<|23.70|> <|23.70|> Don't change a thing.<|24.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> for the end is near and time itself is passing away.<|5.22|> <|5.48|> He's very specific.<|6.38|> <|6.56|> If you're married, stay married.<|7.94|> <|8.76|> If you're unwed, stay unwed.<|11.02|> <|11.74|> And tragically, if you're a slave, don't worry about freedom<|14.78|> <|14.78|> because all of this is about to pass away.<|19.16|> <|19.92|> Stay focused just on the singular moment of right now.<|24.40|> <|24.62|> No plans, no thought for tomorrow.<|26.86|> <|26.86|> No.<|27.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And yet here we are 2,000 years later, and Christ has not literally returned.<|8.76|> <|9.98|> I was talking recently with an 88-year-old who's still an avid hiker and a brilliant mind.<|17.28|> <|17.38|> He remembers everything.<|18.34|> <|19.16|> And he had just returned from the mountains here in Colorado, and we're having a cup of coffee,<|23.64|> <|23.64|> and he said,<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You know, Richard, these mountains are 65 million years old.<|4.60|> <|5.58|> That's the kind of thing you need to tell a southerner every now and then.<|8.54|> <|11.12|> And he was fascinated.<|13.06|> <|13.14|> It was like talking to a kid.<|14.18|> <|14.28|> He was fascinated by his 88 years up against that timeline of 65 million years.<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.62|> Time humbles us in the same way that mountaintop heights humble us,<|7.06|> <|7.16|> making us feel small in a good way as we come face to face<|11.92|> <|11.92|> with our tiny part of this incredible cosmos that is God's.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thankfully, the gospel reading for today is not about the end of the world.<|5.84|> <|6.24|> It pulls in a different direction, a quite different direction.<|9.94|> <|10.60|> For Jesus pairs the return of the master with the return of our hearts, our souls.<|15.94|> <|16.78|> The summary being for where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.<|22.04|> <|22.78|> It's something really practical.<|25.44|> <|26.16|> Amen.<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.72|> This past week, I was also talking with a parishioner named Tamara Destray.<|5.02|> <|5.28|> Tamara was on our vestry recently and is a brilliant parishioner, and she's the director<|11.78|> <|11.78|> of the Conflict Resolution Center at the University of Denver, which is a part of their international<|18.00|> <|18.00|> studies program.<|19.08|> <|19.56|> We're catching up over a cup of coffee and talking about a lot of things and talk about<|24.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> turned, of course, to these most recent tragedies in Dayton and El Paso.<|6.64|> <|7.32|> God rest their souls.<|8.68|> <|10.08|> We talked as well about the desperate need in this country right now<|14.14|> <|14.14|> for bipartisan gun control<|16.22|> <|16.22|> and for what I believe needs to be a ban on automatic weapons,<|20.46|> <|20.64|> which just would, I think, I'm a priest,<|22.88|> <|23.00|> but I think it would make everyone safer, safer,<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> from police to schools and everybody in between.<|4.14|> <|5.84|> Also mused on this gospel reading with Tamara<|10.00|> <|10.00|> and how it seems to be a call to open our hearts,<|15.28|> <|15.52|> open our houses, open our minds<|18.34|> <|18.34|> in a way that feels like disarming spiritually.<|24.66|> <|26.14|> Disarming spiritually.<|27.82|> <|27.82|> Disarming spiritually.<|28.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And Tamara said, "Yes."<|3.20|> <|3.20|> And she said in her work, what she's learned is that every person in one way or another<|10.64|> <|10.64|> is armed.<|12.46|> <|12.46|> And we're armed, she says, in the face of fundamental insecurity.<|17.18|> <|17.18|> And Tamara says, "If we don't talk about that, we're not getting deep down enough<|22.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to talk about why there are such vast differences in our country,<|3.90|> <|4.08|> why we reach such different conclusions about what we should do<|7.94|> <|7.94|> in the face of what we all see, insecurity.<|13.68|> <|15.32|> And that seems to me to be about right.<|19.38|> <|19.38|> Right.<|20.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As a society, we build, we legislate, we protect, we count the cost, we make plans.<|9.80|> <|10.48|> All of these things are normal and even good things to do.<|13.76|> <|14.06|> I mean, a cathedral itself takes the long view of time.<|19.38|> <|19.50|> These things are built over centuries, right?<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And yet, all of us at the end of the day, at the end of this day, all of us face what is unknown and uncontrollable.<|13.46|> <|14.92|> And in light of this gospel reading, and especially in light of Christ's presence in our lives,<|20.72|> <|20.74|> over the long haul, through all the twists and turns of the seasons of our life in the cosmos,<|25.62|> <|26.20|> we're going to be a little bit more.<|26.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The unknown and the uncontrollable can become a means of grace, even a door to the divine.<|8.88|> <|12.58|> Teilhard de Chardin was a paleontologist, of all things, and a Roman Catholic priest who<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And he has great advice for this moment in our time when we come face to face with what is unknown and unpredictable.<|7.90|> <|9.12|> And he says this, he says,<|11.60|> <|11.60|> Trust in the slow work of God.<|17.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> trust in the slow work of God,<|2.86|> <|3.10|> and accept the anxiety of feeling yourself in suspense and incomplete.<|9.64|> <|10.68|> If you like that quote, we put it in the inserts.<|13.46|> <|13.60|> You can take it home with you.<|14.92|> <|16.00|> And that too seems about right to me.<|19.44|> <|20.16|> Looking into the eyes of the uncertainty and unknowns of our life,<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and trusting that as surely as we meet Christ in the sacraments of baptism and Holy Communion,<|7.48|> <|8.32|> so too we will meet Christ in unexpected times and unknown places.<|15.44|> <|16.14|> Which means that faith, the Christian faith especially, is not the opposite of doubt.<|24.88|> <|25.52|> It's the opposite of certainty.<|27.86|> <|28.66|> Right?<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.12|><|endoftext|>
